# COMP110 Data Analysis Project

<img src="/LaylaHunter.github.io-COMP110ex09/Static1/imgs/logo.png" alt="Image of Comp110 rainbow logo. "  width="500">

## Summary of Project 

This project explored whether prior programming experience impacts a student’s self-reported understanding of course material in COMP110. The goal was to determine if students with more experience tend to understand the material better, and whether this suggests a need for course adjustments. The data used comes from student survey responses, specifically focusing on prior experience and understanding levels.

 After organizing the data, we focused on the relationship between 'prior_exp' and 'understanding'. To visualize this, We created a scatter plot, box plot, and line plot. 


<img src="/LaylaHunter.github.io-COMP110ex09/Static1/imgs/plot1.png" width="500">

This plot shows individual students understanding levels across different experience categories.

<img src="/LaylaHunter.github.io-COMP110ex09/Static1/imgs/plot2.png" width="500">

This plot shows the average understanding for each level of prior experience.


<img src="/LaylaHunter.github.io-COMP110ex09/Static1/imgs/plot3.png" width="500">

This plot compares the distribution of understanding for each experience group. It helps show the median and spread of scores.


## Conclusion and Recommendations

The goal of this analysis was to analyze the data given to us and determine whether prior programming experience improves the students understanding of the material in these courses. The graphs partially support this idea. The box plot visual shows that students with more prior experince tend to have a higher median of understanding scores, while students with little or no experience have lower scores. The line graph also shows an upward trend in understanding as students with prior experince increases. However, the scatterplot tells us that there is still overlap between the groups we created,which means some students with little experience were still able to achieve a high understanding. These findings suggest that prior experience is helpful, but is not the only factor that influences student understanding in the course. 

Although the data for prior experience isn't the only reason students have a higher understanding of the material, it supports it. Therefore, I would recommend that the course consider ways to better support students with different levels of experience. However, there are some potential costs and trade-offs to this approach, such as creating different learning tracks, which could make the course more difficult to manage for instructors and TA's due to more planning and teaching resources needed. Another thing to consider would be the type of prior experience that students are coming in with. There is no specification determining what information each student knows, only the time frame in which they have previously coded or the previous courses they have taken. Also, this could potentially lead to some students feeling discouraged in the course by being placed in a specific track.

Future improvements could include collecting more detailed data on the type of prior experience students have, such as particular concepts they are familiar with. This may allow more targeted support and a better understanding of ho prior experience influences learning outcomes. It may also be beneficial to hold targeted office hours or tutoring that address practice and review of core concepts that are hard to wrap your head around if you have never coded before.
